text: The camera is positioned at least fifteen centimeters away from the inferior aspect of the hernia defect to facilitate the incision of the peritoneum, ensuring adequate mesh overlap during the preperitoneal hernia repair.
The surgeon utilizes three robotic instruments: a ProGrasp forceps, monopolar scissors, and a suture cut needle driver, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the hernia repair procedure with lysis of adhesions, reducing the hernia content, and clearing the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon observes that the colon is adhered to the anterior abdominal wall, noting the strong grip strength of the adhesion. The ProGrasp instrument is used cautiously to minimize contact with the bowel, and a fenestrated bipolar is preferred for grasping the bowel if necessary.
The surgeon identifies old mesh from a previous operation and decides to explant it, which creates a visible peritoneal defect that may affect the pre-peritoneal hernia repair approach.

The surgical field reveals the extent of the hernia recurrence. The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision at least five centimeters below the inferior aspect of the recurrent defect, preparing for further exploration and repair.

The surgeon develops a preperitoneal flap, carefully staying within the preperitoneal plane around the central defect left by the mesh explantation. This technique is similar to that used in tap inguinal hernia repairs.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum to ensure adequate coverage for re-peritonealization of an uncoated mesh. The partner palpates the subxiphoid plane to maintain orientation, revealing a large preperitoneal pocket.

The surgeon sizes the defect, measuring three by six centimeters, using surgical instruments. An OV-lock absorbable suture on a CT-I equivalent needle is prepared, with the needles skeeted to fit through an eight millimeter trocar.
The pneumoperitoneum is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury as the surgeon closes the primary defect and places the mesh within the surgical field.

A 15x15 cm sheet of Prolene soft mesh is deployed into the preperitoneal space. The mesh is secured to the abdominal wall using secure straps, avoiding placement high in the abdomen where counter pressure is not possible. The mesh is then re-peritonealized, fitting into the space like a jigsaw puzzle.
